Most SAST tools flood you with alerts. Aikido doesn’t. We surface only real risks and help you fix them fast, inline, in your IDE, or through AI-generated pull requests.

declarative & imperative prompting

my own style has shifted more and more from imperative, chain-of-thought-y to declarative ever since reasoning models came out. in practice this means explaining *what* i want changed instead of *how*, and letting the model figure it out

then i also think some models are just more compatible with you than others, depending on its coding behavior, output style, verbosity etc. i've seen this first hand at cursor where some people have vastly different daily drivers

a steerable model with good infrastructure (like cursor) will allow you to get higher quality output with less input over time. cursor can infer codebase preferences and preserve user preferences with memories. just like a great engineer
